content: Many experienced Rust developers fall into the trap of assuming that because sonic-rs uses SIMD intrinsics to parse JSON, it must be a drop-in replacement for serde_json without any behavioral changes. This is a dangerous misconception because while sonic-rs offers significantly faster parsing speeds thanks to its vectorized operations on character codes and length calculations, it fundamentally breaks the API contract with standard Rust serialization libraries. When you migrate from serde_json to sonic-rs, your existing deserialization code that relies on trait bounds like Deserialize or specific error types will immediately fail because sonic-rs does not implement these traits by default; instead, it exposes raw bytes and custom error enums requiring you to manually construct a Deserialize implementation for every struct you want to parse. The most common gotcha arises when trying to handle malformed input: while serde_json provides the graceful `SerdeError` enum with detailed context strings, sonic-rs returns a generic `sonic::Error` variant that often lacks the human-readable message required by debug logging or user-facing error handling unless you explicitly implement the `Display` and `Debug` traits for your custom wrapper. This forces developers to write boilerplate error conversion code everywhere they encounter JSON parsing failures, which defeats the purpose of using a high-performance crate if it complicates error propagation in production systems. A second critical mistake involves misunderstanding how sonic-rs handles null values; unlike serde_json where `null` is mapped directly to an optional type or `Option<T>`, sonic-rs treats null as a specific byte sequence that must be explicitly checked before attempting to deserialize into a concrete value, meaning you cannot simply use the standard `from_value` method on Option types without additional conditional logic. For instance, if your schema expects an integer but encounters a JSON null for that field, serde_json will return a specialized error variant immediately, whereas sonic-rs will only indicate that the next token was unexpected unless your code explicitly inspects the byte representation of the null terminator before proceeding to parse the remainder of the document. Another subtle pitfall emerges when optimizing performance: developers often assume that because sonic-rs is faster at parsing, it should also be faster at deserializing complex nested structures, but in reality, the overhead of manually implementing trait bounds and error handling can negate the raw parsing speed gains if your code structure does not account for the extra validation steps required. The correct mental model is to treat sonic-rs as a specialized low-level parser that requires you to build a complete serialization layer on top of it; do not expect it to magically solve your library design problems or eliminate the need for proper error handling patterns. Instead, use sonic-rs only when you have verified through benchmarking that the raw parsing time savings outweigh the development and maintenance costs of writing custom trait implementations. Finally, consider this analogy: using sonic-rs is like switching from a standard bicycle to a racing motorcycle; while it goes faster on the track (parsing), you cannot simply swap it into your daily commute without adjusting the gear ratios (API compatibility) and ensuring you have the right tires (error handling). If you rush in expecting the same ride experience, you will crash. The only way to succeed is to build a complete set of adapter crates that wrap sonic-rs's raw output into familiar types, ensuring your error paths are robust before you gain any performance advantage from the SIMD optimizations.
